narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 47% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. This prediction draws from key aspects of your profile, like your undergraduate GPA of 3.20—which is very close to the typical student's 3.21—your LSAT score of 26.5, well below the average of 36.7, and your school deciles of 1st in Year 1 and 2nd in Year 3, both much lower than the averages around the 5th or 6th decile. As a White male from an upper-middle-income family planning to study full-time, you're in line with the majority of applicants, where White students make up about 84%, males 56%, and full-time enrollment 93%. The model's decision was most heavily swayed by your Year 3 decile rank, which strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure compared to higher-performing peers. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which similarly tilted things negatively by being notably lower than what succeeds for most. Your Year 1 decile followed closely, reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles. Family income, undergraduate GPA, racial background, and full-time status each played smaller but still failure-predicting roles in sequence, while your gender as male was the one factor that slightly nudged the odds toward passing. Overall, your lower early grades and LSAT stood out as the biggest drags when compared to students who typically pass, whose profiles cluster higher in those areas. To boost your future chances, focus on retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the 37 average, as that's something you can directly improve with preparation. Strengthening your academic deciles through better performance in any ongoing studies or post-grad courses could also help rebuild that foundation. While background factors like family income or demographics are harder to change, excelling in controllable areas like test scores and grades has turned around predictions for many similar students. We encourage you to use this feedback as a roadmap—many applicants reapply stronger after targeted improvements. Keep pursuing your goals; with these adjustments, your profile could align much better with bar-passing success stories.
